title: Bounded-degree product structure question for planar graphs
statement_text: For each $\Delta\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\mathcal{G}_{\Delta}$ be the family of planar graphs of maximum degree $\Delta$. Do there exist functions $t:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$, $d:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$, and $c:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ such that, for each $\Delta\in\mathbb{N}$ and each $G\in\mathcal{G}_{\Delta}$ there exists a graph $H$ of treewidth at most $t(\Delta)$ and maximum degree $d(\Delta)$ and a path $P$ such that $G\subseteq H\boxtimes P\boxtimes K_{c(\Delta)}$?
context_text: Product structure theorems guarantee every planar graph $G$ satisfies $G\subseteq H\boxtimes P\boxtimes K_3$ for some graph $H$ of treewidth $3$ and some path $P$. In several applications it is useful for $H$ to inherit properties of $G$; in this spirit multiple researchers had repeatedly asked the authors whether $H$ can be taken to have bounded maximum degree whenever $G$ does.
paper_contribution: Answers the question negatively: for infinitely many $n$ there exists an $n$-vertex planar graph $G$ of maximum degree $5$ such that any embedding $G\subseteq H\boxtimes P\boxtimes K_c$ with $H$ of treewidth $t$ and maximum degree $\Delta$ requires $t\Delta c\geqslant 2^{\Omega(\sqrt{\log\log n})}$, so no bounded-degree strengthening of the product structure theorem is possible.
notes: The question is stated as a displayed prose block in the introduction without a formal theorem-environment label and is attributed only to 'a number of researchers' without specific names or citations; the paper fully resolves it (negatively) via Theorem 1.
paper_title: Bounded-Degree Planar Graphs Do Not Have Bounded-Degree Product Structure
